rulings.Case Title: PAUL RYAN ASSOCIATES, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. 44 NORMANDIE, LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 5TH AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 1: CROSS DEFENDANT GFDS ENGINEERS' DEMURRER TO 5TH AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT Sustained without leave to amend. Cross-Defendant's request for judicial notice is granted. The Court did not consider the Declaration of Anthony F. Manzo and the attached exhibits in ruling on this demurrer. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, "a demurrer can be used only to challenge defects that appear on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable.") As to the sixth cause of action, the facts pled in the Fifth Amended Cross-Complaint (FAXC) do not establish that GFDS Engineers ("GFDS") owed a duty to Paul Ryan Associates ("PRA"). (See Ratcliff Architects v. Vanir Constr. Mgmt. Inc. (2001) 88 Cal.App.4th 595.) As to the seventh cause of action, the facts pled in the FAXC do not state a cause of action because there was no contract between GFDS and PRA and "the well settled rule in California is that where the primary objective of a transaction is to obtain services, the doctrines of implied warranty and strict liability do not apply." (Allied Properties v. John A. Blume & Associates (1972) 25 Cal.App.3d 848, 855.) As to the eighth cause of action, the FAXC does not state a cause of action because it does not plead that GFDS owed a duty to communicate accurate information to PRA. The FAXC does not plead physical harm to property or that there was a business transaction between GFDS and PRA. (See Friedman v. Merck & Co. ((2003) 107 Cal.App.4th 454, 483-84.) Additionally, the FAXC does not plead with the requisite specificity what misrepresentations were made. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend in light of the fact that PRA has already had one opportunity to amend these causes of action and has not demonstrated that these defects can be remedied by amendment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/MJM

rulings.Case Title: CARMELA ABALON VS. MICHELLE TORRES et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Pltff'S Attendance At Deposition; Request For Reasonable Sanctions And Attorneys' Fees
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 1, DEFENDANTS MICHELLE TORRES AND VICTORIA TORRES' Mtn To Compel Pltff'S Attendance At Deposition; Request For Reasonable Sanctions And Attorneys' Fees Pro Tem Judge Peter VanZandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. GRANTED. Plaintiff Carmela Abalon is ORDERED to appear on January 9, 2014 at 10:00 am or an alternative date agreed upon between counsel to appear for deposition at STRATMAN, PATTERSON & HUNTER, 505 14th Street, Suite 400, Oakland, CA 94612, (510) 457-3440. Plaintiff Carmela Abalon is ORDERED to pay court reporter costs in the amount of $164.50, the filing fee of $60.00 and attorney¿s fees in the amount of $250.00 within 20 days of notice of entry of order on this motion. Failure to comply with this order may subject plaintiff to terminating sanctions upon application by Defendants. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com or a fax to 415.391.8766 with a copy to all other parties by 4pm one day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem Judge hearing the motion and the Pro Tem Judge will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/JPT

rulings.Case Title: LEONARDO DIGIOVANNI VS. ARMANDO LIMON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Determine/Confirm Good Faith Of Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 3: DEFENDANT AMC ENTEMAINMENT, INC. ERRONEOUSLY NAMED AND SUED AS AMC ENTERTAINMENT, INC. DBA AMC LOEWS METREON 16'S Motion To Determine/Confirm Good Faith Of Settlement The motion for good faith settlement is granted. The moving party presents substantial evidence showing that the settlement is in the ballpark within the meaning of Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assoc., (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488. The opposing party failed to meet its statutory burden to present evidence demonstrating that the settlement lacked good faith. See CCP 877.6(d). rulings.Case Title: APPBACKR INC. VS. JASON ASBAHR et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 11: DEFENDANT ED ROBINSON MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS Granted. The Declaration of Ed Robinson in Further Support of Motion to Quash Service of Summons establishes that Mr. Robinson is not a director of the entity that contracted with Plaintiff; rather, he is a director of the contracting entity's parent company. (Robinson Reply Decl., 3.) The Court does not have personal jurisdiction over Mr. Robinson because Mr. Robinson does not have sufficient minimum contacts with the state of California to not offend the traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. rulings.Case Title: CYNTHIA WONG VS. NEURALTUS PHARMACEUTICALS INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Quash/Dismiss And Demurrer
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 12: DEFENDANT UNIVERSITY OF IOWA PHARMACEUTICALS' Motion To Quash/Dismiss The motion to quash is denied. This is a personal injury case brought by a California resident against defendants for personal injury that allegedly occurred in California when she opened a package containing a product allegedly manufactured by UIP. The Court assumes for purposes of this ruling that UIP is an entity of the State of Iowa and is covered by the Iowa Tort Claims Act, Iowa Code §669.1, et seq. (the "Act"). UIP contends that the Act requires that plaintiff file a governmental claim under the Iowa Act, and that the Iowa district courts are the exclusive jurisdiction for suits under that statute, even where a plaintiff is a non-Iowa resident and the act or omission occurred outside Iowa. Iowa Code § 669.4. Defendant contends that this Court lacks personal and subject matter jurisdiction of UIP. This argument is without merit. The Full Faith and Credit Clause does not require this Court to apply the Iowa Tort Claims Act and dismiss plaintiff's claims. (See Nevada v Hall, 440 U.S. 410 (1979).) Doing so would violate California's legitimate public policy of providing a forum for its resident plaintiff to seek redress for her alleged personal injuries to the extent they are caused by UIP. "[S]ister states who engage in activities in California are subject to our laws with respect to those activities and are subject to suit in California with respect to those activities." Hall v University of Nevada, 8 Cal. 3d 522, 524 (1972). California, its residents and taxpayers have a "substantial interest in providing a forum where a resident may seek whatever redress is done [her]." (Id. at 525) Further, "the presence of the evidence and witnesses in California could, of course, mean that plaintiffs if not permitted to proceed in California could find themselves seriously hampered in proving their case elsewhere." (Id. at 526.) Although UIP contends that California and Iowa's policies of filing government claims do not conflict, UIP fails to address the fact that UIP, by application of the Iowa statute, seeks to do more than require plaintiff to file a governmental claim; UIP seeks to require the California resident file her claim in a district court in Iowa. This conflicts with California's policy of providing a forum for Plaintiff here. Nor has Defendant shown why the principles of comity should apply here to give effect to the laws of Iowa and deny Plaintiff the opportunity to seek redress here. The application of comity suggested by Defendant UIP would not -- as UIP contends -- embody the same policy judgments as California's statute. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. =302/MJM =302/MJM

rulings.Case Title: CYNTHIA WONG VS. NEURALTUS PHARMACEUTICALS INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Continuance Of Hearing On Motion To Quash/Dismiss Demurrer
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 12: DEFENDANT UNIVERSITY OF IOWA PHARMACEUTICALS' Demurrer The demurrer is overruled in its entirety. The Court has jurisdiction over the subject matter of each cause of action, and each cause of action sufficiently states a claim against Defendant UIP. This is a personal injury case brought by a California resident against defendants for personal injury that allegedly occurred in California when she opened a package containing a product allegedly manufactured by UIP. The Court assumes for purposes of this ruling that UIP is an entity of the State of Iowa and is covered by the Iowa Tort Claims Act, Iowa Code §669.1, et seq. (the "Act"). UIP contends that the Act requires that plaintiff file a governmental claim under the Iowa Act, and that the Iowa district courts are the exclusive jurisdiction for suits under that statute, even where a plaintiff is a non-Iowa resident and the act or omission occurred outside Iowa. Iowa Code § 669.4. The Full Faith and Credit Clause does not require this Court to apply the Iowa Tort Claims Act and dismiss plaintiff's claims. (See Nevada v Hall, 440 U.S. 410 (1979).) Doing so would violate California's legitimate public policy of providing a forum for its resident plaintiff to seek redress for her alleged personal injuries to the extent they are caused by UIP. "[S]ister states who engage in activities in California are subject to our laws with respect to those activities and are subject to suit in California with respect to those activities." Hall v University of Nevada, 8 Cal. 3d 522, 524 (1972). California, its residents and taxpayers have a "substantial interest in providing a forum where a resident may seek whatever redress is done [her]." (Id. at 525) Further, "the presence of the evidence and witnesses in California could, of course, mean that plaintiffs if not permitted to proceed in California could find themselves seriously hampered in proving their case elsewhere." (Id. at 526.) Although UIP contends that California and Iowa's policies of filing government claims do not conflict, UIP fails to address the fact that UIP, by application of the Iowa statute, seeks to do more than require plaintiff to file a governmental claim; UIP seeks to require the California resident file her claim in a district court in Iowa. This conflicts with California's policy of providing a forum for Plaintiff here. Nor has Defendant shown why the principles of comity should apply here to give effect to the laws of Iowa and deny Plaintiff the opportunity to seek redress here. The application of comity suggested by Defendant UIP would not -- as UIP contends -- embody the same policy judgments as California's statute. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. =302/MJM =302/MJM

rulings.Case Title: HELEN MICKIEWICZ VS. OPERA PLAZA MASTERS ASSOCIATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First-Amended Complaint,
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 15: PLAINTIFF HELEN MICKIEWICZ'S Motion For Leave To File First-Amended Complaint Granted. Plaintiff shall file the proposed Amended Complaint within 10 days of entry of this order. The proposed Amended Complaint alleges that the defendants "negligently owned, maintained, managed, and operated the premises." The Court cannot determine at this stage of the proceeding that the claims against Opera Plaza LP and Opera Plaza Residential Parcel Home Owners Association "arise solely by reason of an ownership interest as a tenant in common in the common area of a common interest development." (Cal. Civ. Code sec. 1365.9.) rulings.Case Title: ARTI DENTERLEIN VS. JANE O'HARA GAMP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 23, 2013, Line 17: DEFENDANTS JANE GAMP'S, PAT ALLEY'S, and SAN FRANCISCO LAW SCHOOL'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Overruled. As to the second cause of action, the First Amended Complaint ("FAC") adequately pleads that Defendants engaged in deceptive practices. In order to adequately plead a violation of Bus. & Prof. Code §17200, the complaint must state facts that establish the practice is unlawful, unfair, or deceptive. (Byars v. SCME Mortgage Bankers, Inc. (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 1134, 1147)(emphasis added.).) In order to show that a practice was deceptive, the complaint must show on its face that members of the public are likely to be deceived. (South Bay Chevrolet v. General Motors Acceptance Corp. (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 861, 878.) The FAC pleads that members of the public were likely to be deceived by misleading and false statements published by Defendants. (FAC, 37.) The FAC adequately pleads that Plaintiff has standing to maintain these claims, as the FAC alleges that Plaintiff was injured by Defendants' misleading statements. (FAC, 37b, "After using the false and misleading statements to get Plaintiff to enroll at SFLS¿") (See Branick v. Downey Savings and Loan Assoc. (2006) 39 Cal.4th 235, 240.) As to the Third Cause of action, the FAC sufficiently pleads facts to establish a violation of Plaintiff's Equal Protection rights. The FAC pleads state action. (FAC, 42c.) The FAC pleads that Plaintiff was discriminated against because of her "race, color, gender, age, and national and ethnic origin." (FAC, 42e.) As to the sixth cause of action, the Court has already ruled that this cause of action is sustained without leave to amend as to Defendants Jane O'Hara Gamp and Pat Alley. (Order re: Demurrer to Complaint dated October 1, 2013.) While Plaintiff could have removed allegations pertaining to this cause of action from the First Amended Complaint, it is not required, as the record in this case reflects that this is not a viable cause of action. Plaintiff's request to amend the FAC to correct an error is denied. Plaintiff must seek leave of court for such amendment through a regularly noticed motion. (CCP § 473(a)(1).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/MJM
